originalTitle: Will UK interest rates go up?
summary: The interest rate set by the Bank of England affects mortgage, loan and savings rates for millions.
content:
The Bank of England is expected to hold interest rates at 3.75% for a fourth consecutive time, keeping them at the lowest level since February 2023.

Rates dropped from 4% in December 2025, and further cuts had been expected in 2026, but the economic impact of the war in Iran means many analysts believe no cuts are likely for the rest of the year.

Interest rates affect mortgage, credit card and savings rates for millions of people.

What are interest rates and why do they change?

An interest rate tells you how much it costs to borrow money, or the reward for saving it.

The Bank of England's base rate is what it charges other banks and building societies to borrow money, which influences what they charge their own customers for mortgages as well as the interest rate they pay on savings.

The Bank moves its benchmark rate up and down in order to keep UK inflation - the rate at which prices are increasing - at or near 2%.

When inflation is above that target, the Bank typically puts rates up. The idea is to encourage people to spend less, reducing demand for goods and services and limiting price rises.

What has been happening to UK interest rates and inflation?

The main UK inflation measure, CPI, has dropped significantly since the high of 11.1% recorded in October 2022 as a result of the war in Ukraine.

It was 2.8% in the year to May 2026 , unchanged from the previous month.

The Office for National Statistics (ONS), which tracks UK inflation, said inflation remained steady because while transport costs rose by the fastest rate since December 2022, that increase was offset by a slowing in the pace of price rises for food.

The US-Israel war with Iran has put up energy and fuel costs around the world and is expected to increase the pace of price rises more generally.

The Bank of England's base rate reached a recent high of 5.25% in 2023. It remained at that level until August 2024, when the Bank started cutting.

Five cuts brought rates down to 4%, before the Bank held rates at its meetings in September and November 2025, before the December cut and further holds in January , March and April 2026.

Supermarkets hit back over pressure to cap price of milk, bread and eggs

The Bank had been widely expected to cut interest rates twice in 2026, with the first drop predicted to come in March or April.

However, the increase in fuel prices and inflation after the outbreak of the US-Israeli war with Iran has upended all of this.

Analysts are guarded about likely moves for the rest of the year but a wait and see approach is dominant among policymakers.

Sustained higher inflation could push rates up, but the weakness of the UK's jobs market and sluggish economic growth in general means this is by no means certain.

Governor Andrew Bailey said the Bank would "continue to monitor the situation and its impact on the UK economy very closely".

"Whatever happens, our job is to make sure that inflation gets back to the 2% target after the initial impact of the war on energy prices has passed," he added.

How the Iran war may affect your money and bills

How high could UK petrol and diesel prices go?

How do interest rate cuts affect mortgages, loans and savings rates?

Just under a third of households have a mortgage, according to the government's English Housing Survey .

About 500,000 homeowners have a mortgage that "tracks" the Bank of England's rate. That means any cut means a reduction in the monthly repayments on their outstanding loan.

An additional 500,000 homeowners on standard variable (SVR) rates rely on their lender choosing to pass on any Bank rate cut.

But the vast majority of mortgage customers - some 87% - have fixed-rate deals. While their monthly payments aren't immediately affected by a rate change, their future deals are.

As of 17 June, the average rate on a new two-year fixed deal was 5.60%, up from 4.83% at the start of March, according to the financial information service Moneyfacts.

For those looking for a five-year deal, the average rate was 5.57%, up from 4.95% over the same period.

The average two-year tracker rate was 4.49%.

About 800,000 fixed-rate mortgages with an interest rate of 3% or below are expected to expire every year, on average, until the end of 2027. Borrowing costs for customers coming off those deals are likely to rise sharply.

You can see how your mortgage may be affected by future interest rate changes by using our calculator:

Bank of England interest rates also influence the amount charged on credit cards, bank loans and car loans.

Lenders can decide to reduce their own interest rates if Bank cuts make borrowing costs cheaper.

However, this tends to happen very slowly.

The Bank base rate also affects how much savers earn on their money.

A falling base rate is likely to mean a reduction in the returns offered to savers by banks and building societies and vice versa.

As of 17 June, Moneyfacts said the average rate for an easy access savings account was 2.53%.

Any cut in rates could particularly affect those who rely on the interest from their savings to top up their income.

What is happening to interest rates in other countries?

In recent years, the UK has had one of the highest interest rates in the G7 - the group representing the world's seven largest so-called "advanced" economies.

In June 2024, the European Central Bank (ECB) started to cut its main interest rate for the eurozone from an all-time high of 4%, until it reached 2% in June 2025.

However, in June 2026, the ECB raised rates to 2.25% as it reacted to rising prices triggered by the Iran war.

The US central bank - the Federal Reserve - has cut interest rates three times since September 2025, taking them to the current range of 3.5% to 3.75% , the lowest since 2022.

President Trump had repeatedly attacked the Fed, and its then chairman Jerome Powell for not cutting rates earlier. Powell has now been replaced by Kevin Warsh who is expected to be more supportive of further
chineseSummary: 英格兰银行设定的利率影响数百万人的抵押贷款、贷款和储蓄利率。
chineseContent:
预计英国央行将连续第四次将利率维持在3.75%，维持在2023年2月以来的最低水平。

利率从 2025 年 12 月的 4% 下降，预计 2026 年将进一步降息，但伊朗战争的经济影响意味着许多分析师认为今年剩余时间不太可能降息。

利率影响数百万人的抵押贷款、信用卡和储蓄利率。

什么是利率？它们为什么会变化？

利率告诉您借钱的成本或储蓄的回报。

英格兰银行的基本利率是向其他银行和建屋互助协会借款收取的费用，这会影响他们向自己的客户收取的抵押贷款费用以及他们支付的储蓄利率。

英国央行上下调整基准利率，以将英国通胀（物价上涨的速度）保持在或接近 2%。

当通货膨胀高于该目标时，央行通常会提高利率。这个想法是鼓励人们减少支出，减少对商品和服务的需求并限制价格上涨。

英国的利率和通胀发生了什么？

英国主要通胀指标消费者物价指数 (CPI) 自 2022 年 10 月因乌克兰战争而创下 11.1% 的高位以来已大幅下降。

截至2026年5月的一年内为2.8%，与上月持平。

追踪英国通胀的英国国家统计局 (ONS) 表示，通胀保持稳定，因为虽然运输成本上涨幅度为 2022 年 12 月以来最快，但这一涨幅被食品价格上涨步伐放缓所抵消。

美以与伊朗的战争推高了世界各地的能源和燃料成本，预计将进一步加快价格上涨的步伐。

英国央行的基准利率在 2023 年达到近期高点 5.25%。该利率一直保持在该水平，直到 2024 年 8 月央行开始降息。

五次降息将利率降至 4%，之后央行在 2025 年 9 月和 11 月的会议上维持利率不变，之后又在 12 月降息，并在 2026 年 1 月、3 月和 4 月进一步维持利率不变。

超市对限制牛奶、面包和鸡蛋价格的压力进行了反击

人们普遍预计央行将在 2026 年降息两次，第一次降息预计将在 3 月或 4 月发生。

然而，美以伊战争爆发后燃油价格上涨和通货膨胀却颠覆了这一切。

分析师对今年剩余时间可能出现的走势持谨慎态度，但政策制定者普遍持观望态度。

持续走高的通胀可能会推高利率，但英国就业市场疲软和经济增长总体疲软意味着这绝不是确定的。

行长安德鲁·贝利表示，央行将“继续密切关注局势及其对英国经济的影响”。

他补充道：“无论发生什么，我们的工作都是确保在战争对能源价格的最初影响过去后，通胀回到 2% 的目标。”

伊朗战争可能如何影响您的金钱和账单

英国汽油和柴油价格能涨到多高？

降息如何影响抵押贷款、贷款和储蓄利率？

根据政府的英国住房调查，不到三分之一的家庭有抵押贷款。

大约 500,000 名房主拥有“跟踪”英格兰银行利率的抵押贷款。这意味着任何削减都意味着未偿还贷款的每月还款额减少。

另外 500,000 名采用标准可变 (SVR) 利率的房主依赖其贷方选择转嫁银行降息。

但绝大多数抵押贷款客户（约 87%）都有固定利率交易。虽然他们的每月付款不会立即受到利率变化的影响，但他们未来的交易会受到影响。

根据金融信息服务 Moneyfacts 的数据，截至 6 月 17 日，新的两年期固定交易的平均利率为 5.60%，高于 3 月初的 4.83%。

对于那些寻求五年期协议的人来说，平均利率为 5.57%，高于同期的 4.95%。

两年平均追踪率为 4.49%。

预计到 2027 年底，每年平均约有 80 万笔利率为 3% 或以下的固定利率抵押贷款将到期。完成这些交易的客户的借贷成本可能会大幅上升。

您可以使用我们的计算器了解您的抵押贷款可能会受到未来利率变化的影响：

英格兰银行的利率也会影响信用卡、银行贷款和汽车贷款的收费金额。

如果银行削减利率使借贷成本更便宜，贷款人可以决定降低自己的利率。

然而，这往往发生得非常缓慢。

银行的基本利率也会影响储户的收入。

基准利率下降可能意味着银行和建房互助协会向储户提供的回报减少，反之亦然。

截至 6 月 17 日，Moneyfacts 表示，便捷储蓄账户的平均利率为 2.53%。

任何降息都可能特别影响那些依靠储蓄利息来增加收入的人。

其他国家的利率发生了什么变化？

近年来，英国是七国集团中利率最高的国家之一，该集团代表了世界上七个最大的所谓“发达”经济体。

2024年6月，欧洲央行(ECB)开始将欧元区主要利率从历史高点4%下调，直至2025年6月降至2%。

然而，2026 年 6 月，欧洲央行将利率提高至 2.25%，以应对伊朗战争引发的物价上涨。

美国央行——美联储——自2025年9月以来已三次降息，将利率降至当前3.5%至3.75%的区间，为2022年以来的最低水平。

特朗普总统曾多次攻击美联储及其时任主席杰罗姆·鲍威尔没有提前降息。鲍威尔现已被凯文·沃什 (Kevin Warsh) 取代，预计他将更加支持进一步的政策。
whyItMatters: 这条信息属于全球新闻信号，适合用于观察外部环境、政策变化、地缘风险和市场情绪。
